model.label: DeepSeek V3.2
model.description: DeepSeek • General-purpose V-series model optimized for reasoning, coding, and efficient inference.

pick.rationale: Purdue's elite offense, ranked first nationally in adjusted efficiency, should overpower Miami's solid but not elite defense. The Boilermakers' significant advantage in overall team quality and high-major experience from the rigorous Big Ten schedule provides a reliable edge. While Miami is a capable team with a balanced attack, they lack the firepower to keep pace with Purdue's offensive juggernaut over 40 minutes.

reasoningStep.title: West Region Round of 32: Purdue vs Miami Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Evaluated efficiency metrics, team quality, and recent analysis to determine Purdue holds a significant edge due to its elite offense and overall higher ranking.
evidence: Purdue ranks 8th in both Torvik and KenPom with the #1 offense (adjoe 132.6) and a top-36 defense. Miami ranks 30th-35th with a balanced but less dominant profile (offense 35th, defense 45th). Media analysis suggests Purdue is 'red-hot' and a heavy favorite, while Miami's path to an upset would require an exceptional performance against the nation's best offense.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

If they win to continue their March Madness expedition, Miami would likely get No. 2 Purdue in the second round (unless 15-seed Queens University pulls off a massive upset). And it seemed ESPN's Bracketology show would go on the same route. But then former North Carolina guard Jay Williams stepped in to challenge the Duke alum. "I think Purdue will be OK, but Miami's a really good team," Williams said. "Miami's played well. You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.

You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
